name: Disney Audio-Animatronics (Mr. Lincoln)
short_description: A life-sized Abraham Lincoln figure whose face, lips, and limbs move in synchrony with prerecorded speech — the first publicly exhibited humanoid figure to fuse mechanical performance with synchronized vocal expression.
long_description: Debuted at the 1964 New York World's Fair in the State of Illinois pavilion, Great Moments with Mr. Lincoln was the public premiere of Disney's Audio-Animatronics technology. The Lincoln figure, sculpted by Blaine Gibson from a life mask, uses a combination of pneumatic actuators, hydraulic systems, and cable-driven mechanisms synchronized to tones recorded on magnetic tape; each tone triggers a relay that opens a pneumatic valve, producing a specific movement. The figure demonstrated 48 body movements and 17 distinct head and facial cues — including brow furrows, lip movements, and eye direction — timed precisely to Royal Dano's voice performance. It is the origin point of the lineage connecting mechanical theatrical performance to later social robotics and emotive physical AI: the first publicly exhibited life-sized humanoid whose face, lips, and limbs were synchronized to recorded speech at architectural scale.
ai_note: Purely mechanical and electro-pneumatic: solenoids and pneumatic valves triggered by analog tones on prerecorded magnetic tape. No AI, no ML, no adaptive behavior.

images.caption: The Abraham Lincoln Audio-Animatronic figure at Walt Disney: One Man's Dream, Disney's Hollywood Studios, 2017.
images.alt_text: A life-sized seated animatronic figure of Abraham Lincoln in a dark suit, with realistic facial features and a slightly open mouth, displayed in a museum exhibit.

sources.title: Great Moments with Mr. Lincoln
sources.access_notes: Documents the 1964 World's Fair debut, original team (Blaine Gibson, Bob Gurr, Roger Broggie, Marc Davis), figure capabilities (48 body movements, 17 head/facial cues), and Disneyland exhibition history.

sources.title: Audio-Animatronics
sources.access_notes: Technical description of the pneumatic/hydraulic/solenoid mechanism, tape-tone synchronization system, development history from 1949 onward, and trademark history (commercially used 1961, trademarked 1967).
